tikz-graphtheory-dor1-79-9f3db18a: Undirected graph on five vertices A, B, C, D, E (second panel of Fleury's algorithm trace). Edge AD has been removed; remaining edges are B-D, B-E, A-C, C-D, D-E. Current vertex is D; circuit so far is AD.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-80-764edcb5: Same five-vertex graph (third panel of Fleury's algorithm trace). Edges AD and DE have now been removed, leaving B-D, B-E, A-C, C-D. From E only one option remains, so the rest of the Euler circuit ADEBDCA is forced.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-81-fde27163: Undirected graph on seven vertices A, B, C, D, E, F, G drawn left-to-right with B/E above and C/F below. Eleven edges connect the vertices; the reader is asked to decide whether an Euler circuit exists and, if so, to find one.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-82-f1b97b1f: Rectangular 4-by-3 grid graph (twelve vertices, seventeen grid edges, no duplicated edges shown). This is the original graph used to illustrate three different eulerizations in Example 11.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-83-763cb4f9: First eulerization of the 4-by-3 grid: five red bend-left arcs duplicate edges in the middle row and adjacent rows, giving every vertex even degree using five extra edges so an Euler circuit becomes possible.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-84-664706fd: Second eulerization of the 4-by-3 grid using a different choice of five duplicated edges (red bend-left arcs spread across the top, middle, and bottom rows). It also uses five extra edges and yields an Euler circuit.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-85-1cd83454: Third eulerization of the 4-by-3 grid using seven duplicated edges (red bend-left arcs concentrated around the middle column). It is a valid eulerization but uses two more duplicated edges than the previous two examples.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-86-8b8c4c39: Small graph with four vertices A (top), D (center), B (lower-left), C (lower-right) and five edges forming a kite-like shape. Reader must eulerize the graph and then find an Euler circuit on the result.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-87-bd765a35: 4-by-3 grid graph with twelve vertices labelled A-D (top row), E-H (middle), J-M (bottom). Red edges trace the Hamiltonian circuit ABFGCDHMLKJEA, showing a circuit that visits every vertex once but does not use every edge.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-88-52f8addf: Graph on five vertices A, B, C, D, E with vertex E attached to C by a single edge. Because every walk reaching E must return through C, no Hamiltonian circuit exists; Hamiltonian paths such as ECDAB and ECABD do exist.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-89-2ca04aba: Weighted complete graph on five cities (Home/Seattle, LA, Chicago, Atlanta, Dallas) with all ten edges labelled by airfare in dollars (e.g., LA-Seattle $70, Chicago-Atlanta $75, Atlanta-Dallas $85). Used to motivate the Traveling Salesman Problem.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-90-31969058: Weighted complete graph on four vertices A (top), D (center), B (lower-left), C (lower-right). Edge weights: AD=1, AC=2, AB=4, CD=8, BD=9, BC=13. Used to demonstrate the Brute Force algorithm for the TSP.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-91-71192212: Tree diagram of all 24 routes through five cities for the Brute Force TSP. Root is Home (Seattle); next level branches to LA, Dallas, Chicago, Atlanta; subsequent levels enumerate the 4*3*2*1 orderings of the remaining cities.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-92-31969058: Same four-vertex weighted graph (A top, D center, B lower-left, C lower-right; weights AD=1, AC=2, AB=4, CD=8, BD=9, BC=13) revisited to apply the Nearest Neighbor Algorithm.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-93-7dedaa58: Five-city air travel graph (Seattle, LA, Chicago, Atlanta, Dallas) with the NNA tour Seattle-LA-Chicago-Atlanta-Dallas-Seattle highlighted in red. The highlighted total cost is $450, which happens to be optimal here.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-94-31969058: Same four-vertex weighted graph (A, B, C, D with weights 1, 2, 4, 8, 9, 13) used again to illustrate the Repeated Nearest Neighbor Algorithm running NNA from each starting vertex.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-95-3d943dde: Sorted Edges algorithm step 1 on the four-vertex graph: only the cheapest edge AD (weight 1) is highlighted in red.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-96-ea285bd8: Sorted Edges step 2 on the same four-vertex graph: edges AD (weight 1) and AC (weight 2) are now highlighted in red.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-97-7f661b59: Sorted Edges rejected case (BAD) on the four-vertex graph: adding edge AB to the previously selected AD and AC would give vertex A degree 3, so this third edge is rejected.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-98-3d2dcb20: Sorted Edges second rejected case (BAD) on the four-vertex graph: adding edge CD to AD and AC would close the partial circuit ACDA without including vertex B, so CD is rejected.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-99-d7e681ab: Sorted Edges accepted case (GOOD) on the four-vertex graph: edge BD (weight 9) is added to AD and AC because it neither creates a degree-3 vertex nor closes a sub-circuit.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-100-ac5959af: Final Sorted Edges circuit on the four-vertex graph: edges AD, AC, BD, and BC are highlighted, forming the Hamiltonian circuit ACBDA with total weight 25 (not optimal; the optimum ACDBA has weight 23).
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-101-3781f3e0: Empty layout of ten Oregon cities (Portland, Salem, Corvallis, Seaside, Ashland, Newport, Astoria, Eugene, Bend, Crater Lake) drawn as isolated vertices, ready to have Sorted-Edges edges added one at a time.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-102-14ad1e4c: Same Oregon-cities layout after Sorted Edges has added the four shortest edges: Seaside-Astoria (17), Salem-Corvallis (40), Salem-Portland (47), and Corvallis-Eugene (47).
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-103-6242b067: Oregon-cities graph after seven Sorted-Edges selections: the previous four edges plus Portland-Seaside (78), Newport-Eugene (91), and Ashland-Crater Lake (108). Salem and Corvallis already have degree 2.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-104-4d535390: Oregon-cities graph showing the completed Sorted-Edges Hamiltonian circuit: ten edges connect the cities in the tour Portland, Salem, Corvallis, Eugene, Newport, Bend, Ashland, Crater Lake, Astoria, Seaside, Portland (1241 miles).
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-105-9a743118: Weighted graph on six vertices A, B, C, E, F, G arranged left to right with fifteen edges (weights from 11 to 45). Reader is asked to apply the Sorted Edges algorithm to find a Hamiltonian circuit.
